coredata.dc:title: A novel bio-nano emulsion fuel based on biodegradable nanoparticles to improve diesel engines performance and reduce exhaust emissions
coredata.dc:creator: Ettefaghi, Ehsanollah
Ghobadian, Barat
Rashidi, Alimorad
Najafi, G.
Khoshtaghaza, Mohammad Hadi
Rashtchi, Maryam
Sadeghian, Sina
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, energy is the most challenging issue in the world. Although nanotechnology has been promising in offering huge developments in various fields, there are still concerns about the potential threats of nanotechnology products and applications. The concerns are mostly about the possible entrance of nanoparticles into body and their toxicity. As metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles might have toxic effects on living organisms, the use of biocompatible nanoparticles can considerably reduce these concerns. In this research, a combination of diesel-biodiesel-water-biodegradable nanoparticles was used to optimize fuel consumption, reduce pollutants, and enhance diesel engine performance for first time. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were synthesized through the hydrothermal method using orange peel and were then characterized thoroughly. Water and quantum dot nanoparticles were added to the B15 fuel (diesel fuel containing 15% biodiesel) in various concentrations. Then, engine performance experiments were performed using a single cylinder engine to investigate functional parameters and assess exhaust pollutants. The obtained results showed that the addition of water and quantum dot nanoparticles to the B15 fuel causes an increase in engine torque and power and decreases the brake specific fuel consumption. This is due to the better mixing of the fuel and air in combustion chamber and provision of more oxygen. It was also revealed that the use of emulsion nanofuels decreases the emission of unburned hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxides (NOx). By employing the B15W5-CQD60 fuel (B15 fuel containing 5% water and 60 ppm carbon quantum dots), diesel engine power at the rate of 2700 rpm was increased by 21% compared with the B15 pure fuel. Furthermore, using the B15W10 fuel (B15 fuel containing 10% water), NOx emission was decreased by 25% on average compared with the B15 pure fuel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofuel
Emulsion fuel
Biodegradable nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Diesel engine
Exhaust emissions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles
Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
Power, torque, and brake specific fuel consumption of diesel engine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of the steps of conducting the experiments.
Engine and dynamometer set-up.
Chemical structure of carbon quantum dots.
XRD pattern of carbon quantum dots.
Infra-red spectra of carbon quantum dots.
(A) TEM image and (B) particle size distribution of CQDs.
Emulsion fuels of diesel-biodiesel-water containing 1, 5, and 10 vol.% of water.
Engine power and torque for different bio-nano emulsion fuel samples.
Brake specific fuel consumption for different bio-nano emulsion fuel samples.
NOx emission for different bio-nano-emulsion fuel samples.
HC emission for different bio-nano-emulsion fuel samples.
CO emission for different bio-nano-emulsion fuel samples.
Physicochemical properties of diesel and biodiesel fuels.
Features of the single-cylinder diesel engine.
Specifications of the AVL DITEST GAS 1000 pollution analyzer.
Composition of different samples of emulsion fuels.
Comparison of means of main effects on engine's power (kW).
Comparison of means of main effects on engine's torque (N.m).
Comparison of means of main effects on BSFC (g/kWh).
Comparison of means of main effects on NOx pollutant (ppm).
Comparison of means of main effects on HC pollutant (ppm).
Comparison of means of main effects on CO pollutant (vol.%).

head.ce:title: A novel bio-nano emulsion fuel based on biodegradable nanoparticles to improve diesel engines performance and reduce exhaust emissions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical & Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-336, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), P.O. Box: 14665-1998, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, K. N. Toosi University of Technology, 15 Pardis St., Tehran 1999143344, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: K. N. Toosi University of Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Mechanical & Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-336, Tehran, Iran.
Corresponding author. Department Nanotechnology Research Center, Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI), P.O. Box: 14665-1998, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, energy is the most challenging issue in the world. Although nanotechnology has been promising in offering huge developments in various fields, there are still concerns about the potential threats of nanotechnology products and applications. The concerns are mostly about the possible entrance of nanoparticles into body and their toxicity. As metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles might have toxic effects on living organisms, the use of biocompatible nanoparticles can considerably reduce these concerns. In this research, a combination of diesel-biodiesel-water-biodegradable nanoparticles was used to optimize fuel consumption, reduce pollutants, and enhance diesel engine performance for first time. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were synthesized through the hydrothermal method using orange peel and were then characterized thoroughly. Water and quantum dot nanoparticles were added to the B15 fuel (diesel fuel containing 15% biodiesel) in various concentrations. Then, engine performance experiments were performed using a single cylinder engine to investigate functional parameters and assess exhaust pollutants. The obtained results showed that the addition of water and quantum dot nanoparticles to the B15 fuel causes an increase in engine torque and power and decreases the brake specific fuel consumption. This is due to the better mixing of the fuel and air in combustion chamber and provision of more oxygen. It was also revealed that the use of emulsion nanofuels decreases the emission of unburned hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxides (NOx). By employing the B15W5-CQD60 fuel (B15 fuel containing 5% water and 60 ppm carbon quantum dots), diesel engine power at the rate of 2700 rpm was increased by 21% compared with the B15 pure fuel. Furthermore, using the B15W10 fuel (B15 fuel containing 10% water), NOx emission was decreased by 25% on average compared with the B15 pure fuel.

ce:section.ce:para: Internal combustion engines are widely utilized as the main users of fossil fuels. The pollutants which are emitted from internal combustion engines affect the atmospheric condition and cause serious problems such as global warming, air pollution, acidic rains, and breathing problems. Diesel engines are a type of internal combustion engines which have various applications in heavy and light vehicles. The main drawback of diesel engines is the emission of undesirable pollutant gases during fuel combustion. Nitrogen oxides (NOx), unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), carbon oxides (COx), sulfur oxides (SOx), and smoke are among the most important pollutants of diesel engines. Up to now, many researchers have attempted to produce a suitable combustion system to completely burn hydrocarbons. However, the issue still remains challenging [1–3]. Enhancing the fuel quality and providing a better combustion of fuel in engines are among the methods considered for solving this problem. In these methods, there is no need the make changes in engine structure. The use of biofuels, water, nanoparticles, and other additives is among the solutions that have been investigated for enhancing combustion and fuel quality [4–9].

Biodiesel fuel correspond to a class of vegetable oils which have been esterified from agricultural products which contain oil. Considering the properties of biodiesel which highly resembles diesel, biodiesel can be used along with diesel fuel in diesel engines, enhancing the engine performance in some cases [10–12]. Also, the use of diesel-water emulsion in diesel engines can increase combustion efficiency and reduce pollutant emission [13–15]. Another method which has been of great importance in recent years is the use of nanomaterials to enhance the properties of diesel fuel. In fact, the use of nano additives in fuel can assist the completion of the combustion process, in turn reducing pollution and enhancing engine performance. Up to now, various metallic, metal oxides, and carbon nanoparticles have been employed in studies on nanofuels [16–18]. For instance, Bidita et al. [19] studied the effect of adding water to diesel fuel on engine performance. They utilized the Triton X-100 surfactant to stabilize the water inside the diesel fuel. In their research, water was added to diesel fuel in different volume concentrations of 0.5–0.9 and the prepared emulsions were stable for two weeks. Their results showed that the sample containing 0.9 vol.% water led to the highest decrease in pollutant emission and provided the highest brake power and thermal efficiency. Annamalai et al. [20] investigated the pollutant emission and performance of diesel engines by incorporating biodiesel containing cerium oxide (CeO2) nanoparticles. The main goal of their research was to decrease the emission of pollutants from the exhaust. Their fuel was composed of 5% water, 93% biofuel, 2% Span80, and 30 ppm CeO2 nanoparticles. Their results showed that the addition of water to fuel decreases NOx and smoke emission and enhances the brake thermal efficiency. Furthermore, they reported that the addition of CeO2 to fuel reduces CO, HC, NOx, and smoke. Also, Mirzajanzadeh et al. [21] synthesized a catalyst composed of CeO2 nanoparticles/CNT hybrid to enhance the performance of diesel engines and reduce the emission of CO, HC, NOx, and smoke. They added the prepared catalyst to the mix of diesel-biodiesel (B20 and B5) in various concentrations of 30, 60, and 90 ppm. They reported that the addition of nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel improves the combustion process toward complete combustion and decreases the emission of pollutants, including CO, HC, NOx and smoke, by 38.8, 71.4, 18.9, and 26.3%, respectively. Moreover, Jung el al [22]. demonstrated that adding cerium nanoparticles to diesel fuel considerably reduces the combustion temperature and increases the oxidation rate. Sadhik Basha and Anand [23] also added aluminum nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes in the concentrations of 25 and 50 ppm in combination of diesel-biodiesel to enhance engine performance and reduce pollutants emission. In addition, Chandrasekaran et al. [24] studied the effect of nano additives on diesel-biodiesel. They employed copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles in 50 ppm concentration as an additive and reported that the brake thermal efficiency of the nanofuel was enhanced by 2.2% compared with the base fuel. Also, the emission of HC, CO, and smoke was substantially decreased. In a research performed by Sajeevan and Sajith [25], the effect of adding a mix of CeO2 and zirconium nanoparticles on diesel fuel properties and the emission of smoke was studied. Ultrasonic waves and oleic acid (C18H34O2) were utilized to stabilize the nanoparticles in concentrations of 2.5–20 ppm. They reported that smoke emission was decreased by 31% for the nanofuel and the thermal efficiency was increased by 3%. Despite the attempts made in the field of fuel additives, there are still many unsolved problems in this regard. The use of water at high concentrations results in the reduced heating value of the fuel as well as damage to the engine. Besides, the use of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles can lead to the environmental hazards. Also, the production of high-stability nano-emulsion fuels and the high cost of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles are among other common problems in earlier studies. So in the present research for first time, by producing the diesel-biodiesel-water-bionanoparticles compound, it was attempted to reduce the problems of the additives while benefiting from their advantages.

In general, the development in nanotechnology and its various applications have introduced inevitable interactions between nanomaterials and the environment. On the one hand, studies show that nanoparticles have positive effects on the performance of various physicochemical systems, and on the other hand, there are serious concerns regarding the emission of metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles to the environment [26]. In fact, since common metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles can remain in different environments without any physicochemical changes for a long time [27], there are many concerns regarding their potential threats to human health and environment [28]. Hristozov and Malsch [29] studied the hazards of engineered nanoparticles for the environment and human health. Also, Joo and Zhao [30] reviewed the factors that affect the fate, transformation, and toxicity of metal oxide nanoparticles.

Quantum dots are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials which have attracted the attention of researchers. Carbon quantum dots were accidentally discovered by Xu et al. while purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [31]. In addition to having fluorescent properties, quantum dots have advantages including low toxicity, being environmental friendly, low cost, and simple preparation methods. Quantum dots can be synthesized using biocompatible low-cost materials such as ethanol, citrate (citrate acid salt), glucose amine, ascorbic acid, watermelon peel, fruit peel, orange juice, strawberry juice, sugarcane, eggs, gelatin, and many other biocompatible materials. Due to their unique electrical, optical, and mechanical properties [32,33] and low production cost [34], quantum dots have been considered for many applications, including chemical sensors [35], biosensors [36,37], bio-imaging [38], nanomedicine [33], and optical applications [39–41]. Sahu et al. [42] selected orange juice for preparing carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. They proposed a single-step and simple method for synthesizing carbon quantum dots. In this method, 40 mL of orange juice was under the hydrothermal condition for 150 min at 120 °C. They reported preparing 400 mg of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles with high fluorescent properties through this method.

Beside the advantages of nanoparticles, there are concerns about their potential threats to human health. As metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles might have toxic effects on living organisms, using bio-nanomaterials can decrease these concerns considerably. In this regard, a combination of diesel-biodiesel-water-bio-nanoparticles was used to optimize fuel combustion, reduce the emission of pollutants, and enhance diesel engine performance, in this research. As quantum dots have excellent stability in aqueous solutions, water was used as an intermediate fluid to stabilize the nanoparticles inside the biodiesel fuel. Quantum dot nanoparticles and water were added to the B15 fuel in different concentrations. Then, engine experiments were performed to investigate the functional parameters and assess the emission of pollutants from exhausts using a single-cylinder engine.

The steps of conducting the experiments are shown in Fig. 1 . Nano-emulsion fuels were prepared in three steps. In the first step, distilled water was added to the diesel fuel. Then, in second and third steps, nanoparticles and biodiesel were added to the prepared solution, respectively, to obtain the final product. Afterwards, experiments were performed to explore the performance and emission of pollutants by employing a single-cylinder, four-stroke engine. In this research, the Euro 4 diesel fuel was purchased from fueling stations in Tehran, Iran, and used as the base fluid for preparing the nano-emulsion fuel. The biodiesel was prepared through the transesterification method from the waste of cooking oil in laboratory. The initial properties of diesel and biodiesel fuels utilized in this research are depicted in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dot nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were prepared through the hydrothermal method using orange juice. To this end, 1 g of orange peel was powdered and, with 40 mL of water, transferred to an autoclave with a steel body and a Teflon container. This solution was under hydrothermal conditions for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling the autoclave body until reaching the room temperature, the precipitates were separated from the solution by centrifugation at the rate of 5000 rpm. The mentioned separated solution contained carbon quantum dots, was dried at 80 °C, and labeled as CQD. Next, to characterize the prepared nanostructures, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were incorporated. The X-ray diffraction patterns of CQDs were recorded using a Philips PW 3710 instrument (30 KV, 35 mA, Cu Kα) to examine the formed phases. FTIR spectrum was obtained by a Perkin Elmer Spectrum-GX instrument over the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm−1 to study the functional groups over the nanoparticles. The morphology of samples was explored by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss, EM900). Finally, the size distribution of CQDs was studied by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique.

As mentioned previously, nanofuels were prepared in three steps. Water was used as the intermediate fluid because carbon quantum dots are highly stable in it. Then, biodiesel and nanoparticles were added to the fuel. To stabilize the water in the diesel fuel, fatty acids were employed as the lipophilic agent and their neutral salt was used as the hydrophilic agent. In one case, to prepare 100 cm3 of emulsion, 10 vol.% of diesel-water (diesel containing 10 vol.% water), 7 cm3 of oleic acid as fatty acid, along with 0.7 cm3 of monoethanolamine were poured in a container with the capability of being stirred at the rate of 500–1000 rpm. After a short while and the neutralization of a part of acid, a hydrophilic phase (fatty acid salt) was formed and part of it remained in the reaction environment as a hydrophobic phase. Then, 81.3 cm3 of diesel fuel, 10 cm3 of water, and 1 cm3 of n-hexanol as solvent assist were added to the previous solution and, after a short period of stirring (about 1–5 min), water droplets were distributed throughout the homogenous fuel diesel as nanoscale phases, and a highly transparent and stable diesel-water emulsion was obtained. The emulsion fuels were prepared in three concentrations of 1, 5, and 10 vol.%. Then, 15 vol.% of biodiesel was added to the emulsion fuel. Finally, quantum dot nanoparticles in three concentrations of 20, 60, and 100 ppm were added to the emulsion fuel and the bio-nano emulsion fuel was prepared.

To investigate the functional parameters of the engine, including power, torque, and brake specific fuel consumption, and assess the emission of pollutants such as NOx, HC, and CO, a set of experiments was performed. For this purpose, a single-cylinder diesel engine which was joined to an eddy current dynamometer was utilized. The set-up of the diesel engine and dynamometer is illustrated in Fig. 2 . An air-cooled single-cylinder engine manufactured by Lombardini (Italy) was incorporated and its features are shown in Table 2 . The used dynamometer was of eddy current which can evaluate the maximum power of 21 horsepower, maximum rate of 10000 rpm, and maximum torque of 80 N m. This dynamometer evaluates torque level and rotational speed by applying magnetic field. Also, temperature sensors were embedded on the engine to monitor the temperatures of the engine oil, gases exiting the exhaust, and the environment. The engine and dynamometer were equipped with a central monitoring system by which the parameters were monitored and recorded.

To evaluate the emission of pollutant gases from the exhaust, an AVL DITEST GAS 1000 pollution analyzer made by AVL (Germany) was used. This instrument can detect and evaluate gases including NOx, HC, CO, CO2, and O2 with a high accuracy. The accuracy and detection range of these gases by the analyzer are depicted in Table 3 . It should be noted that all experiments were performed in a full-load state in four engine rates of 1800–2700 rpm, at room temperature (30 °C), and at atmospheric pressure. Also, temperature of the engine oil was set at 80 °C at the onset of all the tests.

In this part, the results of the synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots and the preparation of nano-emulsion fuels are first presented. Next, results obtained from engine performance experiments are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots are generally spheroidal nanoparticles consisting of an amorphous nucleus up to nanocrystals with dominant graphite (carbon SP2) or graphene and oxidized graphene sheets or diamond-like hybrid carbon (SP3). Fig. 3 shows a schematic representation of the chemical structure of quantum dots. Quantum dots mostly contain 1-20 wt% of oxygenic groups in the form of carboxylic (COOH), hydroxyl (OH) groups, etc. Using nitrogen-containing precursors, amine groups in the form of NH, CN and NH2 can be formed in carbon quantum dots. As mentioned previously, quantum dots have desirable properties such as low toxicity, being environmentally friendly, low cost, and simple synthesis methods.

The XRD pattern of the prepared carbon quantum dots is illustrated in Fig. 4 . In this figure, the graphite specific peak related to (002) plane can be observed at
2 θ
of 25° and, according to Bragg equation, its d-spacing is 0.36 nm. This peak is wide and the distance between the planes of this sample is more than that of graphite, indicating the presence of functional groups which lead to a decrease in the number of layers and quantum dots size. Furthermore, the presence of functional groups on quantum dots was analyzed considering the FTIR spectrum. Fig. 5 demonstrates the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized sample. The adsorption band at 1601 cm−1 is due to the CC bond. Also, two other adsorption peaks in the 1600-1680 cm−1 region correspond to CC and CN bonds, and these peaks overlap. The adsorption peaks at 3414, 2929, 1386, 1281, and 1100 cm−1 are related to OH, CH, CO, and CO bonds, respectively, in the synthesized sample. Therefore, functional groups composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen were formed over the quantum dot structure. Fig. 6 shows the HRTEM image and DLS results of the synthesized CQDs. As can be seen, the CQDs have dot morphology with the mean size of about 1–3 nm.

As explained, the first step of preparing the nano-emulsion fuel is adding water to the diesel fuel. In general, to produce a stable emulsion with regard to interfacial tension, balance should be established between aqueous and hydrocarbon phases. Surface reactive materials which can be solved in aqueous or hydrocarbon phases singly cannot reduce the interfacial tension and form a nano-emulsion. The most important factor in the preparation of a stable nano-emulsion is the balance between aqueous and hydrocarbon phases. The nanoscale particles of water are dispersed throughout the hydrocarbon phase due to using and mixing two surface reactive materials. One of the surface active materials can be solved in water and the other one can be solved in hydrocarbon. By mixing the surface reactive materials, the interfacial tension is decreased and approximately reaches zero. As a result, a stable nano-emulsion of water in hydrocarbon can be prepared. The surface reactive lipophilic or hydrophobic materials which can be solved in hydrocarbon fuels include fatty acids with carbon chains of 12–20 and alkylphenols in which the alkyl group is composed of 8 or more carbon atoms and has the property of balance between hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity in the range of 1–8. Also, the hydrophilic surface reactive materials are composed of alkylene polyamines or alkanolamines with fatty acids which possess the balance level of 12 between hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity. As explained in Materials and Methods, to stabilize and disperse water nanoscale particles inside the diesel fuel, oleic acid was used as the lipophilic agent and neutral salt was utilized as the hydrophilic agent. Different emulsion fuels were prepared as depicted in Table 4 .

As diesel and biodiesel can be mixed easily and form a homogenous phase, in the next step, 15 vol.% of biodiesel was added to the diesel-water emulsion and a transparent and stable diesel-biodiesel-water emulsion was obtained by stirring for a short while. Fig. 7 shows different nano-emulsion fuels containing various amounts of water in the B15 fuel. As can be observed, the prepared samples are of high transparency and stability. In the third step, carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were added to the diesel-biodiesel-water emulsion in different concentrations (20, 60, and 100 ppm). Due to the presence of many functional groups over the quantum dots, these particles could be dispersed and stabilized well in water. After adding nanoparticles to fuel, the container of the sample was kept in bath ultrasonic for 5 min. Due to their ultra-small size (less than 10 nm) and having many functional groups, quantum dots could easily be dispersed in water droplets inside the emulsion and a transparent, stable emulsion was obtained. The stability of the prepared emulsion nanofuel was investigated and monitored visually over time. The results showed that, after 6 months, no precipitates were formed and there was no change in the phase of the samples.

The prepared samples were labeled as B15Wn-CQDm in which B15 shows the base fuel (diesel fuel containing 15 vol.%), W denotes water, n indicates the volume fraction of water in the sample, and m corresponds to nanoparticle concentration in the sample.

The effect of independent parameters, including engine speed, nanoparticle concentration and water volume fraction, on dependent parameters, including power, torque, and brake specific fuel consumption, and also the emission of pollutants (i.e. NOx, HC, and CO) while using the diesel-biodiesel-water-nanoparticle fuel were studied. To analyze the obtained data, a factorial design in the form of a completely random design was used and repeated three times. The experiment data were interpreted by the analysis of variance (ANOVA) and comparison of means on the results was performed. In what follows, results of each variable are investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Power, torque, and brake specific fuel consumption of diesel engine
ce:section.ce:para:
ANOVA results showed that parameters of engine speed, nanoparticle concentration and water volume fraction have significant effects (i.e. around 1%) on the power, torque, and specific fuel consumption of diesel engine. Results of the comparison of means of the main effects on power and torque of the engine are presented in Tables 5 and 6
. Also, the results of power and torque obtained for the nano-emulsion fuel are presented in Fig. 8 . Although the addition of water to the B15 fuel in concentrations of 1 and 10 vol.% reduced the engine power and torque, 5 vol.% of water increased the engine power and torque. This is because the addition of water to fuel on the one hand decreases its thermal value which has a negative effect on engine torque and power, and on the other hand introduces the micro-explosion phenomenon which has a positive effect on engine power and torque. Therefore, it can be stated that, in the sample containing 5 vol.% of water, the effect of micro-explosion phenomenon dominated the decrease in thermal value and, consequently, the engine power and torque were increased. In fact, the micro-explosion phenomenon is the secondary atomization process of the injector's output mixture due to the rapid evaporation of water in the emulsion droplets. Once the emulsion is sprayed in a hot environment such as combustion chamber, the heat reaches the surface of the emulsion droplet. As the water particles' temperature reaches the super-hot temperature, the rapid expansion of the water bubbles causes the expansion and explosion of the entire emulsion droplet; consequently, the fine particles of gasoline are formed. It should be noted that the micro-explosion phenomenon assists in the better mixing of fuel and air and leads to a complete combustion process. It was also reported that the presence of water in fuel causes a delay in ignition and a higher pressure is provided in the dead point over the piston which increases engine power and torque [43]. Bulent Koc and Abdullah [44] also reported an increase in engine power and torque for the emulsion containing 5 vol.% of water. Variables such as water droplet size in the emulsion, temperature and pressure inside the cylinder, fuel type, type of surface reactive materials, and type of diesel engine used in experiments affect the variation of engine power and torque, which are generally different in various studies.

The results also revealed that the addition of CQD nanoparticles to the fuel increased the engine power and torque compared with the B15 pure fuel and water-biodiesel emulsions. The best result was obtained for the sample containing 60 ppm nanoparticles. An increase in engine power and torque due to the addition of CQD nanoparticles can be attributed to the fact that nanoparticles lead to the better mixing of fuel and air by increasing the contacting surface, and that the presence of oxygenic functional groups over the surface of nanoparticles can compensate for the oxygen lack in the combustion chamber. Therefore, the combustion process occurred better, in turn increasing the engine power and torque. Similar results have also been reported by other researchers [21–24, 45]. According to the results, the highest engine power belonged to the B15W5-CQD60 sample at the rate of 2700 rpm which was increased by 21% compared with the B15 pure fuel. Also, another notable point is that the nanofuel's performance declines by increasing the nanoparticles' concentration up to 100 ppm, which can be due to the agglomeration of CQD nanoparticles as the result of increased concentration.

Results of the comparison of means test of the main effects on BSFC are presented in Table 7 . Also, the values of brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) for the nano-emulsion fuel are presented in Fig. 9 . BSFC is calculated via dividing the overall fuel consumption by engine power at a specific speed (rpm). As mentioned previously, the addition of CQD nanoparticles at 60 ppm concentration causes an increase in engine power due to various reasons. Therefore, brake specific fuel consumption is decreased in comparison to pure fuel. Considering the effect of water addition on BSFC, it can be stated that the micro-explosion phenomenon due to the presence of water droplets causes a better mixing of the fuel and air and, as a result, a better fuel combustion occurs which decreases BSFC. This phenomenon is more significant in samples containing 5 vol.% of water. Based on the results, the lowest BSFC value corresponded to B15W5-CQD60 at the rate of 1800 rpm which was decreased by 3% compared with the B15 pure fuel.

ANOVA results showed that engine speed, nanoparticle concentration and water volume fraction have significant effects (i.e. around 1%) on the emission of NOx, HC, and CO. Results of comparison of means of main effects on NOx pollutant and the relevant values are presented in Table 8 and Fig. 10 , respectively. In general, the main factor leading to the production of nitrogen oxides in engines is the high temperature of combustion chamber. At higher temperatures, O2 molecules are decomposed to O atoms which produce nitrogen oxide in presence of nitrogen. Therefore, by decreasing the local temperature of the combustion chamber, the emission of nitrogen oxides can be controlled. Based on the results, the amount of water has a direct effect on reducing NOx emission. By increasing the amount of water to 10 vol.%, the NOx emission was decreased significantly. The NOx emission for the B15W10 sample was decreased by 25% on average compared with the B15 pure fuel at different speeds. This is due to the high latent heat of water which adsorbs the heat in the cylinder during the vaporization of water particles, leading to a decrease in the local temperature of the combustion chamber. Similar results were reported regarding the effect of diesel-water emulsion on NOx emission [19,44, and 46]. The results also revealed that the addition of CQD nanoparticles decreases the NOx emission. Because the presence of functional groups on the nanoparticles leads to further completion of the combustion process and, consequently, reduction in production of NOx. Overall, it can be stated that using emulsion nano-fuel decreases the NOx emission from the engine exhaust. The lowest NOx emission was about 137 ppm for the B15W10-CQD20 sample at 1800 rpm.

Results of comparison of means of main effects on HC pollutant and the relevant values are presented in Table 9 and Fig. 11 , respectively. In general, the main factor leading to the production of HC pollutant in the combustion chamber is incomplete combustion phenomena. In fact, unburned hydrocarbons are part of the fuel which have not participated in the combustion process and are emitted from the engine unburned. Thus, by providing the conditions of a complete combustion, HC emission can be reduced. The results show that the addition of water and CQD nanoparticles in optimized concentration leads to a better combustion and reduces the HC emission. In other words, a better mixing of the air and fuel due to the micro-explosion phenomenon and the presence of oxygenic functional groups over the nanoparticles lead to a better combustion process and decrease the HC emission. This is observed for nano-emulsion fuels containing 5 vol.% of water and different concentrations of nanoparticles. The lowest HC emission was about 55 ppm corresponding to the B15W5-CQD60 sample at 1800 rpm.

Also, results of comparison of means of main effects on CO pollutant and the relevant values are presented in Table 10 and Fig. 12 , respectively. Regarding the CO emission, the results did not follow a regular trend, and different results were obtained for different nano-fuel samples. However, it is evident that the addition of CQD nanoparticles led to an increase in CO emission which can be attributed to the oxygenic functional groups over quantum dots.

In this research, carbon-based bio-nanomaterials were prepared and used as fuel additives to reduce the concerns regarding the application of metallic and metal oxide nanoparticles as additives in diesel fuel. Biodegradable carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were employed as additives for the biodiesel fuel to decrease the serious problems regarding toxicity, stability, and economic issues. In this regard, to optimize fuel consumption, reduce pollutant emission, and enhance diesel engine performance, a combination of diesel-biodiesel-water-nanomaterials was used in this research. Carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were synthesized using orange peel and, along with water, were added to the B15 fuel in various concentration of 20, 60, and 100 ppm. The results showed that the addition of water and carbon quantum dot nanoparticles in optimized concentrations leads to an increase in diesel engine power and torque and reduces the brake specific fuel consumption. This is due to the better mixing of the fuel and air in the combustion chamber and providing more oxygen for a better combustion. Regarding the reported results, the optimized concentrations of water and nanoparticles are 5 vol.% and 60 ppm, respectively. The diesel engine power at 2700 rpm was increased by 21% for the B15W5-CQD60 sample compared with the B15 pure fuel. Also, the application of bio-nano emulsion fuels reduced the emission of unburned hydrocarbons and nitrogen oxide which is attributed to a complete combustion process. Finally, it is proposed to investigate the economic analysis of the bio-nano emulsion fuel as well as its effect on the emission of the soot pollutant in future studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Tarbiat Modares University and especially the Research Institute of Petroleum Industry (RIPI) of Iran for providing equipment to doing researches.
